subtopic: Payment Systems, Government Borrowing, and RBI Initiatives
title: Uniform Asset Classification System
focus: The rule for consistent classification of Non-Performing Assets (NPAs) among co-lenders.
rationale: This section covers a specific and examinable rule regarding NPA classification by co-lenders, a key aspect of banking regulation.
sectionExcerpt: The uniform asset classification system mandates that if one lender in a consortium tags a loan as a Non-performing Asset (NPA), all other co-lenders must also classify it as an NPA.
evidenceBullets: If one lender tags a loan as Non-performing Asset (NPA), other co-lenders must do the same.

subtopic: Payment Systems, Government Borrowing, and RBI Initiatives
title: Payment Aggregators
focus: The definition, legal structure, and regulatory framework for Payment Aggregators (PAs) as entities facilitating merchant payments.
rationale: This section defines a key component of the digital payments ecosystem. Questions can be framed on the function of PAs, their legal incorporation, and the mandatory RBI authorization for non-bank entities.
sectionExcerpt: A Payment Aggregator (e.g., Paytm, Google Pay) is an entity that facilitates the aggregation of payments from customers to merchants. It must be incorporated under the Companies Act, 2013, and the new framework mandates that non-bank PAs obtain RBI authorisation under the Payment and Settlement Systems Act, 2007.
evidenceBullets: It is an entity that facilitates aggregation of payments made by customers to merchants.
It is incorporated as a company under the Companies Act, 2013.
New framework mandates non-bank PAs to obtain RBI authorisation under the Payment and Settlement Systems Act, 2007.

subtopic: Payment Systems, Government Borrowing, and RBI Initiatives
title: Ways and Means Advances (WMA)
focus: The mechanism, purpose, legal basis, and terms of Ways and Means Advances (WMA) provided by the RBI to governments.
rationale: WMA is a fundamental concept concerning the RBI's role as a banker to the government. The section provides multiple factual points on its purpose (not for financing fiscal deficit), legal backing, interest rate, and duration, which are suitable for questions.
sectionExcerpt: Ways and Means Advances (WMA) are temporary advances given by the RBI to the central and state governments to cover mismatches in receipts and payments. Authorized by the RBI Act, 1934, WMAs are not a source of financing the fiscal deficit. The interest rate is the RBI's repo rate, and the advance must be repaid within three months.
evidenceBullets: It is a temporary advance given by the RBI to the central government to cover any mismatch in receipts and payments.
Unlike ad hoc Treasury Bills, the instrument of WMAs is not a source of financing fiscal deficit.
Legal framework: RBI Act, 1934 authorises the central bank for WMA.
Interest rate on WMA is the RBI’s repo rate.
It shall be fully paid off within 3 months from the date of making the WMAs.

subtopic: Payment Systems, Government Borrowing, and RBI Initiatives
title: New Initiatives by RBI
focus: Recent RBI initiatives including the Unified Lending Interface (ULI), the CBDC retail sandbox, and the Tokenisation of Certificate of Deposits (CDs).
rationale: This section covers three distinct and modern financial initiatives by the RBI. Each initiative—ULI for credit, CBDC for digital currency, and tokenization for money market instruments—is a potential source for a question on financial technology and regulation.
sectionExcerpt: The RBI has introduced several new initiatives: the Unified Lending Interface (ULI) as a Digital Public Infrastructure to improve credit delivery; a retail sandbox for Central Bank Digital Currency (CBDC) to allow fintech firms to test solutions; and the Tokenisation of Certificate of Deposits (CDs), which are negotiable money market instruments issued by banks and select FIs.
evidenceBullets: Unified Lending Interface (ULI): It is envisioned as a Digital Public Infrastructure (DPI) aiming to enhance delivery of credit.
Central Bank Digital Currency (CBDC) retail sandbox: For fintech firms to innovate and test solutions.
Tokenisation of Certificate of Deposits (CD): Creating a digital representation, called a 'token', of a real-world asset.
CD is a negotiable money market instrument issued by Scheduled commercial banks with a maturity period up to one year.

subtopic: Payment Systems, Government Borrowing, and RBI Initiatives
title: Self-Regulatory Organisation for NBFCs
focus: The framework for Self-Regulatory Organisations (SROs) for NBFCs, including the recent recognition of FIDC and the eligibility criteria for SROs.
rationale: This section details a significant development in the regulatory architecture for NBFCs. It covers the 'why in news' aspect (FIDC), the legal basis (RBI's Omnibus Framework), and structural requirements for an SRO, providing clear points for questions.
sectionExcerpt: The RBI has granted Self-Regulatory Organisation (SRO) status to the Finance Industry Development Council (FIDC) to oversee NBFCs. This is governed by the RBI’s 2024 Omnibus Framework, which mandates that an SRO must be a not-for-profit company registered under Section 8 of the Companies Act, 2013.
evidenceBullets: RBI granted Self-Regulatory Organisation status to Finance Industry Development Council (FIDC) to oversee NBFCs.
Legal Backing: RBI’s Omnibus Framework for recognising Self-Regulatory Organisations (SROs) for Regulated Entities (REs), 2024.
An SRO shall be setup as a not-for-profit company registered under Section 8 of the Companies Act, 2013.
